switch_i_rmsd.note: Switch I is highly mobile in apo state — samples open and closed conformations

switch_ii_rmsd.note: Switch II shows large fluctuations — pocket opens and closes dynamically

switch_i_rmsd.note: Switch I stabilized by drug binding — much less mobile than apo

switch_ii_rmsd.note: Switch II rigidified by MRTX1133 — locked in closed conformation

binding_pocket_volume_A3.note: Pocket volume stable throughout simulation — drug remains bound

switch_i_rmsd.note: Switch I stabilized similarly to GDP-bound holo state

switch_ii_rmsd.note: Switch II rigidified — MRTX1133 stabilizes similar conformation regardless of nucleotide

switch_ii_stabilization.conclusion: MRTX1133 reduces Switch II flexibility by ~4-fold, consistent with the paper's claim of conformational trapping

switch_i_stabilization.conclusion: Switch I is also stabilized (~2.4-fold), supporting the claim that drug binding affects both switch regions

nucleotide_independence.conclusion: Near-identical Switch II rigidification in GDP and GTP states — strongly supports the paper's claim of nucleotide-state-independent conformational trapping

summary.verdict: GROMACS simulations strongly validate the paper's conformational trapping mechanism. MRTX1133 reduces Switch II flexibility by 4-fold and Switch I by 2.4-fold. The drug-bound conformation is nearly identical in GDP and GMPPCP states, directly confirming the paper's key mechanistic claim that MRTX1133 works through nucleotide-state-independent switch remodeling.
